food_name: Fruit Tea (Taiwanese-style Brewed Tea with Fresh Fruit)
food_subgroup: Brewed tea beverages with fresh fruit

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn term exists for Taiwanese-style fruit tea. FOODON_03301103 (tea beverage) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for fruit-infused brewed tea does not currently exist in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_description: Tea, infusions and other hot beverages
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR covers tea and infusion beverages broadly. No specific code exists for Taiwanese fruit tea with fresh fruit additions. This is the closest defensible parent category in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 (Tea substance) is the closest available anchor. No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for Taiwanese-style brewed fruit tea beverages.

main_ingredients: brewed tea (black or green tea base)
fresh seasonal fruit (e.g. passion fruit, lemon, orange, strawberry, kiwi)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Taiwanese-style fruit tea is approximately 400 g (one large cup), consisting of brewed tea, fresh fruit pieces, sugar syrup, and ice.

nutrient_source.title: Beverages, tea, ready-to-drink, sweetened with fruit juice — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central fruit tea and sweetened tea entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Taiwanese-style fruit tea as served by CHICHA San Chen. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for sweetened brewed tea with fruit juice (NDB 14355 and related entries), adjusted for typical Taiwanese fruit tea composition: brewed tea base (~85% by weight), fresh fruit (~8%), sugar syrup (~7%). Energy and macros reflect a moderately sweetened fruit tea at approximately 30–35 kcal per 100 g. Sodium is low as tea base contains minimal sodium. Sugar content reflects added syrup and natural fruit sugars. Values represent a plausible mid-range estimate for this beverage category.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened tea and fruit juice beverages
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Taiwanese-style fruit tea. GI is estimated at 45 (low category) based on analogous beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database: sweetened fruit juice beverages and lightly sweetened brewed tea beverages typically fall in the GI range of 40–55. The moderate sugar content (predominantly fructose from fruit and sucrose from syrup) and the presence of tea polyphenols, which may attenuate postprandial glucose response, support a low-to-moderate GI estimate. GL per serving calculated as: (45 × 31.2 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 14.0. Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021;114(5):1625-1632.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (~20 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Taiwanese-style fruit tea is very low in sodium (approximately 5 mg per 100 g), making it generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though added sugar content should be monitored as excess sugar intake may contribute to elevated blood pressure over time.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added sugar and total carbohydrate load (~31 g carbohydrate per 400 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Taiwanese-style fruit tea with caution due to its moderate glycemic load (GL ~14 per serving) driven by added sugar syrup; requesting reduced or no added syrup and choosing smaller serving sizes can substantially lower the glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and low sodium; tea polyphenols may confer modest cardioprotective benefit
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Taiwanese-style fruit tea contains negligible saturated fat and very low sodium, and the brewed tea base provides flavonoids and catechins associated with modest cardiovascular benefit; however, regular consumption of the sweetened version contributes added sugar that should be limited in a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taiwanese-style fruit tea (水果茶, shuǐguǒ chá) is a cold brewed-tea beverage assembled with fresh seasonal fruit, a sweetened syrup base, and ice. It is a signature product of Taiwanese tea-house chains such as CHICHA San Chen, which serves it in Singapore under the name 'Bao Dao Classic Fruit Tea' (宝岛经典水果茶). The drink typically uses a black or green tea base brewed to a light-to-medium strength, combined with sliced or whole pieces of fresh fruit such as passion fruit, lemon, orange, strawberry, or kiwi, and sweetened with cane sugar syrup or honey. The result is a refreshing, lightly sweet, mildly astringent beverage with bright fruity aromas and a translucent amber-to-golden colour. The texture is liquid with floating fruit pieces. Nutritionally, fruit tea is a low-energy beverage at approximately 32 kcal per 100 g, with the majority of calories derived from carbohydrates (predominantly sugars from the syrup and fruit). Protein and fat content are negligible. The beverage provides a modest amount of vitamin C from the fresh fruit and small quantities of potassium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low category), though the glycemic load per standard 400 g serving is moderate at approximately 14, driven by the total sugar content. Tea polyphenols in the brewed base may contribute antioxidant and mild cardioprotective effects. Clinically, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should moderate intake due to added sugar; those with hypertension will find the sodium content negligible. The drink is vegan, dairy-free, and gluten-free, making it broadly suitable across dietary restrictions. It is commonly consumed as an afternoon refreshment or social beverage in Singaporean and Taiwanese food culture.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fruit tea?
Is fruit tea suitable for diabetics?
What is CHICHA San Chen fruit tea?
What is the GI of fruit tea?

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry for Taiwanese-style fruit tea exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central sweetened brewed tea and fruit juice beverage entries, adjusted for typical Taiwanese fruit tea composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable sweetened tea and fruit beverage categories. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but not based on a direct laboratory analysis of this specific product.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central sweetened brewed tea entries (NDB 14355 and related) adjusted for Taiwanese fruit tea composition. No exact HPB or USDA entry exists for this specific product. 2. protein_g (0.2) and fat_g (0.1) are very low but non-zero, reflecting trace contributions from the tea base and fruit; they are not zero and do not require zero-macro justification. saturated_fat_g (0.0) is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based beverage with no dairy or animal fat; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central tea beverage entries. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.0) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.0) are confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central tea beverage entries. trans_fat_g (0.0) is confirmed zero as no hydrogenated fats are present in this beverage. cholesterol_mg (0.0) is confirmed zero as this is a plant-based beverage containing no animal products. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central tea beverage source entries used for estimation. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries and is expected to be absent in a plant-based beverage. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. The food_name in the input ('Fruit Tea宝岛经典水果茶') has been normalised to a canonical English name with the Chinese name moved to local_names and aliases. 6. This record is served by CHICHA San Chen at One Fullerton, Singapore, at SGD 7.90. 7. Serving size of 400 g is an estimate for a standard large cup of Taiwanese fruit tea; actual serving size may vary by outlet.

improvement_actions_taken: Normalised food_name from mixed Chinese-English input string to canonical English name.
Moved Chinese name (宝岛经典水果茶 / 水果茶) to local_names and aliases.
Added Malay (Teh Buah), Tamil (பழ தேநீர்), and Hindi (फ्रूट टी) local names to satisfy G6 multilingual compliance for Chinese cuisine served in Singapore context.
Estimated nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central sweetened brewed tea entries with transparent methodology.
Set GI to 45 (low) as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for comparable sweetened tea beverages.
Documented all -1 sentinel values with explicit source-not-reported justifications in notes.
Documented all 0.0 confirmed-zero values (saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg) with confirmed-zero justifications in notes.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all language-specific alias strings appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: closest_parent for all three ontology systems.
Set glycemic_index to positive numeric value (45) — not null or 0.
